data.title: Janus Henderson Contrarian Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned -4.88% and the S&P 500® Index returned -4.33%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the consumer discretionary and healthcare sectors detracted from relative performance. Stock selection in the information technology and industrials sectors contributed.

Outlook

Our differentiated investment approach aims to provide equity diversification for clients, along with returns not tied to an index or the broader economy.

Investment environment

  • The equity market started out the year with modest gains, supported by resilient economic growth.
  • Volatility increased in March, after war in the Middle East drove the price of oil over $100 per barrel. Inflation fears pushed long-term bond yields higher while dampening hopes for Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts.
  • Artificial intelligence (AI) remained a powerful investment theme, with continued share price gains for companies supplying the AI supply chain. Software stocks declined as investors questioned how AI may reshape market dynamics.

Portfolio review

We remain excited about opportunities around AI, and our conversations with companies across the ecosystem have reinforced our view that demand for AI compute will continue to exceed supply for the foreseeable future. This should fuel ongoing capital investment and opportunities for companies, especially those operating in critical bottlenecks around infrastructure, memory, optical, and power. Such companies include Vertiv Holdings, a top relative contributor for the first quarter. Vertiv is an essential supplier of cooling, power management, and rack systems to AI data centers. It has delivered strong earnings growth, with its orders backlog more than doubling on a year-over-year basis. It has continued to expand its market reach and capabilities through strategic investments, especially in liquid cooling technologies, and it announced a new partnership with NVIDIA (NVDA) to develop advanced solutions to support high-density data centers. Shares rose further on news that Vertiv's stock will be added to the S&P 500 Index, which may draw additional investor attention to the stock.

Lumentum Holdings, another outperformer, is a leading supplier of optical components critical to AI-driven data center networking. The company has several avenues for revenue growth, including sales of optical transceivers, which support the transmission of data through optic cables by transmitting electrical signals into light. It also has a strong foothold in the optical circuit switch market, where Google (GOOGL) is a major customer, and a growing business in co-packaged optics, which are replacing copper as a key connectivity material. Demand for Lumentum's optical components has outpaced supply, resulting in tremendous pricing power and robust earnings growth. Adding to investor excitement, NVIDIA announced that it would invest $2 billion in Lumentum to secure future capacity, further highlighting Lumentum's growing market power as a key provider of mission-critical components to data centers.

Oracle (ORCL) was a relative detractor. While Oracle has continued to gain share in the hyperscale market, with a backlog of more than $500 billion, the stock declined in the first quarter on market concerns over the company's ability to fund its ambitious infrastructure buildout. Concerns about potential disruption in Oracle's legacy software businesses also pressured the stock. We believe these concerns are overstated, as Oracle has multiple avenues for funding growth due to its solid balance sheet and strong operating cash flow. Additionally, we believe its software business is relatively insulated from AI competition given its focus on mission-critical systems. We remain invested in the stock.

Flutter Entertainment, another detractor, is a leading online sports betting and iGaming company. It benefits from global reach, with a strong U.S. presence through its ownership of American gambling company FanDuel. Shares fell in the first quarter after Flutter reported disappointing earnings and guidance, reflecting challenges with promotional generosity and slowing handle growth. These results fueled concerns that traditional sports betting may be losing ground to the growing popularity of the sports prediction markets. Flutter is taking steps to bolster its competitive positioning through its launch of FanDuel Predicts, a predictions platform that will leverage its marketing expertise and economies of scale. We continue to see long-term opportunity for Flutter given its strong market position, global diversification, and commitment to innovation.

Manager outlook

We remain constructive on the broader market outlook, despite near-term uncertainty. While we are mindful of the potential economic effects of higher oil prices, we do not expect events in the Middle East to materially disrupt U.S. economic activity. Consumer spending has held up well, particularly among higher-income households supported by asset appreciation. Tax refunds should provide a modest tailwind for consumers, helping to offset the negative impact of higher gasoline prices. More broadly, capital investment is becoming a stronger engine of economic growth, especially in the AI complex, where hyperscalers are expanding their 2026 capital spending plans.

While we continue to monitor these broader themes along with macroeconomic developments, our primary focus remains on bottom-up stock selection. Our differentiated investment process aims to provide broad exposure across segments, sizes, and styles, while avoiding reliance on any single theme. Through this process, we seek to unlock value through differentiated insights, especially around misunderstood business models, undervalued assets, and unappreciated growth potential. We remain on the lookout for opportunities to take advantage of market volatility, as our long-term investment horizon and independent process allow us to capitalize on opportunities other investors might miss. Through this approach, we hope to provide equity diversification for clients, along with returns not tied to an index or the performance of the broader economy or investment market.

data.title: Intellia Therapeutics And Wall Street Continue To Play Their D&D Game - Data And Doubt
data.description:
I wonder how many people (besides me) have never played Dungeons & Dragons, commonly known as D&D.

That famous role-playing game debuted in 1974. But back then, I was more interested in disco. During that year, Disco broke beyond its underground heritage into the mainstream when “Billboard created its first ‘Disco Action’ chart on October 26, 1974.”

Like many fads, disco eventually died. Many date that from a 7/12/79 Disco Demolition Night event at Chicago’s Comiskey Park. Between games of a baseball doubleheaders, the promoters blew up a crate of vinyl disco records.

D&D, however, kept on going — on and on and on and even into the present. By 2024, about 50 million people are said to have played D&D. And with the game having migrated beyond tabletop toward electronic, it’s still marching on.

I’m not sure how many in the investment community play Dungeons & Dragons. How many have the spare time?

I didn’t mourn the death of disco in 1979. I, too, developed other pressing demands on my time. I started my first stock analysis job at Value Line (VALU) on 12/1/79.

It seems, though, there’s another D&D game that’s become hot — at least between Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA) and the analysts-investors who follow or trade in the stock. I call it Data & Doubt.

This new type of D&D game is not (yet) commercially available. But the company and investor teams have been paying for a whie. And the competition really heated up in the past few days

I’ll set the stage by introducing the players and their respective teams…

Team Data — Intellia Therapeutics

I’ve covered NTLA often (most recently on 11/19/25). I’ve done that because I’m fascinated by the company’s methods of developing drugs. It does so using the Crispr Therapeutics (CRSP) gene-editing platform.

This, to me, sounds like the future of medicine.

My wife tells me my ailments are caused by my failure to do what she keeps telling me to do. But scientists suggest genetic factors (gene mutations, gene damage, etc.) are more likely at fault.

It seems logical to assume if we can repair (EDIT) genes, we’d have more substantive permanent cures than we get with medications that merely mask underlying conditions. See generally here and here for more on gene editing.

The latter link is to my first 8/3/24 NTLA writeup. There, I discussed the two approaches to gene editing. Ex vivo means the curative agent is created outside the body from genetic material extracted from the patient at a special treatment center, and then the agent is injected into the patient. In vivo means the curative agent is injected into the patient, in a doctor’s office.

Lately, NTLA has been and still is about two in vivo programs.

Team Doubt — Investors

Normally, one would expect that good data (including news) about a company would lead to better stock performance. But often, this doesn’t happen.

There’s the traditional buy-on-the-rumor-sell-on-the-news phenomenon.

But there’s also just, plain doubt, a tendency to try to poke holes in any sort of positive picture a company paints.

To a degree, we have to respect the latter. It goes directly to the heart of what many would consider good analysis.

But good things taken to excess can turn out bad. Differentiating between good analysis and doubt taken to excess is one of our most challenging tasks.

Recent D&D Events

Round 1: October 2024, Lonvo-z

I described my first exposure to an NTLA-versus-Investors D&D episode on 10/30/24.

I wrote then about the stock having reacted to good clinical-study news about NTLA-2002, now known as Lonvo-z, and its ability to cure HAE.

This is a very serious condition that causes severe and painful swelling. Depending on where it occurs, say, in the patient’s airways, it can lead to death.

This condition is often diagnosed at about age 20. And as of now, it lasts for life — so far…

Lonvo-z aims to correct the genetic defect that causes HAE. If successful, sufferers will be able to replace ongoing often multiple times per week treatments with a one-time doctor’s office infusion.

Back in ’24, NTLA disclosed very successful Phase 2 clinical test results.

Wall Street reacted with a selling blitz —NTLA shares immediately plunged 20.5%. My 10/30/24 article explained why I believed, as I wrote in my headline, that investors’ reasons for bearishness were “flimsy.”

In sum, I believed analysts thought themselves better-suited to evaluate sampling and treatment efficacy than the experts at the FDA. The latter deemed the test results good enough to warrant allowing Lonvo-z to move on the Phase 3 testing. Analysts seemed unconvinced.

Essentially, that whole episode struck me as similar to what football fans encountered in connection with last week’s National Football League (NFL) draft of college players. Looking at sports media and social media, it seemed as if everybody knew better than experienced NFL general managers, scouts and coaches which players teams should draft!

Team Doubt got the first points. But then, the stock recovered and then some. So…

The Round 1 winner - Team Data.

Round 2: July-October 2025, Nex-z

Formerly known as NTLA 2001, this drug aims to treat ATTR. This refers to improperly folded proteins in the liver that can cause all sorts of painful and sometimes dangerous, and possibly fatal, blockages.

Data started out seeming to be fine. But in July 2025, one patient was found to have undesirably high levels of a particular liver enzyme. He was asymptomatic and the condition soon resolved on its own. Phew…

The initial Round 2 winner — Team Data but it was a close come-from-behind victory.

But then, in October 2025, tragedy struck. A study participant experienced troublesome symptoms. His clinical-study manager directed him to go to a hospital. He did. And he passed away shortly thereafter.

Between voluntary action on the part of NTLA and FDA mandate, the Nex-z trial ground to a halt.

There are good reasons to assume Nex-z was not the cause of death. The 80-something patent had other complicating comorbidities.

Further investigation ensued. Additional safety measures (e.g., tighter eligibility screening requirements, additional monitoring) were implemented.

For more details, see my 11/19/25 report.

Subsequently, on 3/2/26, the FDA lifted the clinical hold on testing. NTLA has since gone back to enrolling study participants.

Given the FDA’s go-ahead, it’s tempting to declare victory in favor of Team Data. Supporting that is the reality that drugs on the market that play valuable roles in public health might still be fatal to certain users. (Example: I’d like but can’t get GLP-1 medications since for me, the risk of pancreatic cancer is high.)

But I won’t do that. The facts that surfaced as they surfaced were dire. It would have required an incredible combination of naiveté and callousness to have taken them in stride. So…

The ultimate Round 2 winner - Team Doubt by a landslide

Round 3: Now (April 2026), Lonvo-z

On Friday 4/24/26, NTLA dropped 14%. Then, for a while in the aftermarket, the stock was up around 20%.

At about 8 am on Monday 4/27/26, management delivered another update on Lonvo-z, this one covering Phase 3 test results.

I thought the news was terrific.

Apparently, so too did at least 10 of the 16 participating analysts who, before asking questions, explicitly congratulated NTLA on the excellence of the reported results.

So I assume there would be no way we could repeat the experience of Round 1, when the stock reacted to good data by plunging. This time, the stock would surely hold its aftermarket gains, and move higher when trading opened.

I was wrong.

Actually, most of the post 4/24/26 gain evaporated by the time the market opened on 4/27/26. And the stock closed the day with a 4.3% loss. As of this writing, about an hour into after-market trading, its down another 2%.

So as it appears for now…

In the lead - Team Doubt

Deconstructing Round 3 — The Data

Here’s the number of participants’ HAE attacks per month during the study.

The Lonvo-z and the placebo groups both experienced quick and substantial drops in the number of HAE attacks per month. But as one would expect (hypothesize) at the outset, the drop was much more significant for the Lonvo-z group.

The placebo group, as time passed and the emotional impact of thinking they had Lonvo-z gave way to medical reality, experienced increasing attack frequency.

Look now at what happened after the crossover period (marked by a vertical dashed line). This is when the placebo folks were given the real Lonvo-z. Notice the vigorous and fast decline in their attacks per month, a pattern that essentially mirrored what we saw at the outset for the actual Lonvo-z patients.

I doesn’t take a statistics PhD to recognize that Lonvo-z is making a difference. And the “side effects” experiences of the Lonvo-z and placebo groups were minor and similar to one another.

Here’s an overall summary.

The left portion shows the mean number of monthly attacks. NTLA supplies some significance data on the image. But do you really need it? Anyone should recognize, simply by eyeballing, how excellent that result is.

The right side offers a different perspective. It shows during the meat of the study period (weeks 5 through 28), the percent of participants that were completely attack-free. Again, eyeballing alone should make it clear that this is great.

Deconstructing Round 3 — The Doubts

As with the reaction to Phase 2, analysts again started looking to pick things apart. But this time, the questions didn’t strike me as off base as they did in Round 1. And management’s answers seemed reasonable.

But the stock’s April 24-27 declines tell me the doubts, even if not boldly expressed, remain present. So we’re left to guess and infer as best we can.

It could be a simple buy-the-rumor-sell-the-news situation.

But my sense is that analysts are still looking for more perfection than may be feasible.

For example, one analyst wondered why the 62% of patients being attack free in weeks 5-28 wasn’t higher, and how things would look in the real world when patients know they aren’t in a clinical test.

The answer was…

I think you're referring to the attack-free rate of 62%. So it's a challenging endpoint, meaning that you basically have to be perfect that no one reports any HAE symptoms at all. And that can be -- it's a high bar in part because HAE symptoms are so variable. And most -- really all studies have relied on patient-reported outcomes. So by that, I mean, we know HAE, for instance, causes abdominal attacks. And so if a patient reports abdominal pain suggestive of an HAE attack, the investigator must have to adjudicate that. But we tend to trust our patients. And if they report that as an HAE symptom, we review it, but it may be reported as such, even if there are other possible explanations for that abdominal pain.

So that's all to say that the symptoms are highly variable. And so any symptom that is suggestive of HAE or receives HAE treatment during the course of the study period will likely be recorded as an HAE attack. So I personally think that's the reason that we don't see 100% attack-free in really any of the trials that have been done even with ver effective medicines…. The second part of your question, how will it perform in the real world? I can't predict the future. But what I can tell you is that nearly all of the HAE preventative therapies that we prescribe now have generally outperformed in the real world compared to the clinical trial.

That’s reasonably representative of most of the clinically-oriented questions asked on the update call.

Beyond that, as I listened to the call live earlier in the day, I sensed particular concern about the desirability of having a gene-edited treatment at all. How many patients were really unhappy with existing treatment protocols?

I’m not sure the number of such questions was exorbitant. My sense may have been influenced by the vehemence with which they were expressed, and by my own belief that this line of questioning was, indeed, ridiculous.

I wonder about the ages and health of the analysts on the call. Have any of them had medical issues that required lifetime multiple-times-per-week treatments?

We don’t have to quiz HAE patients as to how they feel about hypothetical (to them) alternatives. Common sense alone should tell us a one-time doctor’s office infusion will be much preferred, even if sufferers today who don’t yet have the ability to choose it don’t yet realize what their answers will be in the future, when things get real.

Round 3 Outcome

As with Round 1, I’m casting my vote for Team Data. I think the Phase 3 results were stupendous. And I see the doubts as having ranged from neutral to absurd.

NTLA sees it this way. That’s why it’s preparing for a commercial launch of Lonvo-z in the first half of 2027.

This will require finalization of a rolling BLA. This is Biologics License Application that is submitted in phases, as various regulatory milestones are completed. Right now, NTLA is on track for finalization early next year.

But my opinion won’t influence the stock. And so far, neither has clinical data nor the FDA’s opinion. Mr. Market is the final arbiter. And so far…

In the lead - Team Doubt

Go figure.

Risk

Obviously, with any clinical stage biotech, there’s a ton of clinical execution risk. This isn’t exclusive to NTLA. It’s part and parcel of the business.

So, too, is financial risk. We know for sure companies like this can’t stay in business unless they keep getting outside capital.

That’s exactly what NTLA has been doing.

Ignore the revenues. Those are non-commercial research-like payments. Focus on the cash. It came down from its peak. That’s no surprise. Companies like NTLA burn cash and will continue doing so until commercial operations get going.

They could fund themselves by borrowing. I’m glad, however, NTLA hasn’t gone that route. The last thing in the world a company like this needs is fixed interest-expense burdens.

So NTLA has kept going back to the equity markets to sell more stock. That’s the right approach for it. As long as suppliers of such capital remain confident in a company’s future, they’ll keep the money spigot flowing.

And by the way, when it comes to raising equity, please, please, please do yourself a favor and ignore a lot of what you see from time to time from those who complain about dilution. This is not a simplistic computation. You can’t just add more shares to the calculation. You also have to consider how much extra profit the company will eventually earn from the new capital. (That’s the hard part many commentators ignore.)

Ultimately, NTLA management believes it now has enough cash to get to mid 2027. And I believe it will get more when it needs it, especially since that would be the time when Lonvo-z will be starting to go commercial. If Next-z is back on it’s feet by then, so much the better.

What to do About NTLA Stock

NTLA does not yet have the sort of commerce-based fundamentals that can support use of conventional valuation metrics.

So a decision today on the stock depends on how Mr. Market, as judge and referee, is assessing the contest between Team Data and Team Doubt.

The price chart gives us some insight here.

Looking at the 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) and the 50-day EMA, we see that Mr. Market has lately had a soft spot in favor of Team Data (as expressed by the 10-day EMA being higher). But it’s been only mild and inconsistent.

Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO) are more definitive. Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Both indicators had some bullish moments in the recent past. For now, they’re solidly ensconced in the bearish Team Doubt camp. Sellers are more motivated than buyers.

But I’m not inclined to call it a day and stop here. We’ve seen how things in this news-driven stock can turn on a dime. Team Doubt has been strong lately. But I can see Team Data putting up a much tougher fight in the months ahead (due to more progress toward commercializing Lonvo-z, and Nex-z putting more distance between it and its recent woes).

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

Right now, I see stalemate for NTLA in Team Data versus Team Doubt. That suggests neutral market performance.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by continuing to rate NTLA as a “Hold.”

data.title: Best Dividend Aristocrats: April 2026
data.description:
2026 Review

The Dividend Aristocrats had a terrible March collectively, and are thus far not enjoying as robust a rebound as the broad equity market. The early outperformance the Aristocrats have built on the S&P 500 has all but faded here in April. What's also interesting is the range of returns for individual Aristocrats. In March the margin between the best Aristocrat, Exxon Mobil (XOM), and the worst, McCormick (MKC), was in excess of 40%. In April thus far this gap is equivalently high, except now Exxon is the worst performing Aristocrat, with Nucor Corporation (NUE) leading the charge.

The ProShares S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrat ETF (NOBL) was down 7.04% in March and is up 2.04% through April 27th. While the S&P 500, measured by the SPDR S&P 500 Trust ETF (SPY), was 4.93% down in March, and is up 9.91% in April thus far.

Despite losing their momentum relative the SPY, 32 individual Dividend Aristocrats are still outperforming SPY this year, with 23 sitting on double digit gains.

Promising Aristocrats Right Now

What has already happened is in the past and it is not relevant to a long-term focused investor. What is important, is what the market is presenting us with today and where do we think individual stocks may be headed in the distant future. My framework, as described below, is not meant to be a direct "buy now list", but rather a starting point aimed at fleshing out opportune investment ideas within the Dividend Aristocrat universe of stocks.

Valuation is assessed using dividend yield theory, which compares the current dividend yield to the 5-year rolling average. The potential long-term return is calculated as the sum of expected future earnings growth, the forward dividend yield, and a potential boost from mean reversion. Momentum is measured using a combination of the 3-, 6-, and 12-month price movement, excluding the last month in the 12-month figure. I measure a composite momentum score and compare that to SPY.

The idea behind the momentum composite score is that stocks with strong positive momentum may be more likely to offer positive upside in the short term. It is just another piece of information that can be used to time a more opportune entry point for a long-term position.

data.title: Pagaya: Capitalizing On Private Credit's Great Rotation While Operating Leverage Shines
data.description:
Since reporting Q4 earnings, shares of Pagaya Technologies Ltd. (PGY) is down 27% after missing top-line estimates for the first time in 7 quarters and posting conservative guidance for FY 2026. Pagaya's earnings results and conservative guidance were driven by the company tightening its box and becoming more selective in its approvals due to the uncertainties brought by the ongoing geopolitical instability and inflation fears.

Despite this uncertain environment, Pagaya's operating leverage continues to shine as the company reported sequential improvements to its net margin and free cash flow in Q4 despite shedding $100-150 million in network volume. In my opinion, the scale of the company's operating leverage could drive earnings upside this year, especially as its exposure to non-performing loan vintages from 2023 and prior continues to decline as these vintages approach maturity.

Meanwhile, Pagaya's robust underwriting, evidenced by its high repeat borrower rate, is a key differentiator amidst the ongoing state of the private credit market. With private credit rotating into consumer loans to reduce their exposure to direct corporate lending's jitters, the company could see strong momentum for its ABS transactions and potentially secure additional forward flow agreements. As is, the company appears to be benefiting from this market dynamic, with its ABS issuances this year seeing a wave of new investors.

Although Pagaya's fundamentals remain robust, expanding its partner network and guiding for strong bottom-line growth in FY 2026, it still trades at a discount relative to its peers, suggesting significant reversal potential in the near term. In light of these factors, I'm reiterating my strong buy rating for Pagaya with a base case $23 price target, implying 68% upside potential from current levels.

Operating Leverage Offsets Credit Tightening

In late Q4 2025, Pagaya decided to tighten its box and reduce its exposure to higher-risk segments such as single-family rentals, which had zero volume in the quarter, and borrower pools. This decision was not due to deteriorating credit performance but due to the company's lending partners' lower risk appetite amidst the ongoing geopolitical uncertainties and inflation fears.

In fact, the credit performance of Pagaya-enabled loans was better than management's forecast in FY 2025 as credit-related impairments for the full year came at $95.4 million. This figure was lower than management's forecast between $100-150 million and 76.9% lower than the $412.1 million incurred in the prior year. While the credit-related impairments still represented 11.3% of Pagaya's average loan balance in FY 2025, $845.5 million, it should be noted that the company is still exposed to the non-performing vintages of 2023 and prior.

At the end of FY 2025, the company held $181 million in 2023 and prior loan vintages, representing 19.2% of the loans held on its balance sheet. As these loans reach their maturities, Pagaya could be set to report significantly lower credit-related impairments. With that in mind, cumulative net losses (CNL) for personal loan vintages from the second half of 2024 through the first half of 2025 are trending 30-40% lower than peak levels in 2021, while auto loan CNLs over the same period are trending 50-70% lower than 2022 vintages, per management in the Q4 earnings call.

Still, Pagaya's decision to tighten its model had a material impact on its Q4 earnings results as it shed $100-150 million in network volume in December and anticipates the decision to represent a $1.5 billion headwind to its network volume in FY 2026. This decision saw the company miss top-line estimates for the first time in 7 quarters while also marking its first sequential network volume decline during its seasonally strongest quarter since 2022.

During the quarter, Pagaya's network volume declined 4.2% sequentially from $2.8 billion to $2.68 billion, driving a 5.5% decline in fee revenue from $339.9 million to $321 million as its take rate slightly contracted from 12.1% to 12%. In addition, Pagaya's fee revenue less production costs (FRLPC) declined 6% over the same period, from $139.3 million to $131 million, outpacing its fee revenue decline, as production costs decreased at a slower pace compared to fee revenue at 5.2%, falling from $200.6 million to $190 million.

The main contributor to the slower decline in production costs is that 50% of the company's fee revenue came from products other than its core Decline Monetization product. These products typically carry a lower FRLPC margin profile as they are still new products and haven't reached Decline Monetization's maturity yet. As a result, Pagaya's FRLPC as a percentage of network volume came in at 4.9% in Q4, down from 5%, the higher end of management's target, in the prior quarter.

Despite the top-line pressure brought by Pagaya's tightening, its operating leverage shone bright in Q4. During the quarter, the company's core operating costs, which include technology, data, product development, sales and marketing, and G&A, declined 6.6% sequentially from $69.6 million to $65 million, outpacing the top-line decline, and represented 49.7% of its FRLPC compared to 50% in Q3.

Pagaya's operating leverage saw it report a relatively flat operating income sequentially, despite the top-line decline, at $79.7 million compared to $80 million, representing a 23.8% operating margin compared to 22.8% in the prior quarter, a 100 basis point expansion. In turn, the company was able to report a 52.1% sequential increase to its GAAP income from $22.5 million to $34.3 million. This represents a 10.2% net margin, a significant expansion from 6.4% in the prior quarter.

These efficiencies reflected on Pagaya's operating cash flow, which increased 19% sequentially from $67 million to $79.8 million as it converted nearly 61% of its FRLPC into operating cash flow compared to 48% in the prior quarter. In this way, the company generated $76.7 million in free cash flow in Q4 at a 22.9% margin, 20% higher than Q3's $63.8 million at an 18.2% margin.

The scale of Pagaya's operating leverage is set to be the primary driver for its bottom-line growth in FY 2026. In its full-year guidance, the company anticipates full-year network volume to grow 15.1% YoY at the midpoint to $12.13 billion, outpacing revenue, which management anticipates to grow 14.3% at the midpoint to $1.49 billion. This suggests that the company is positioned to see a decline in its take rate, which would see its FRLPC as a percentage of network volume trend near the midpoint of its 4-5% target.

Despite this, Pagaya is guiding for adjusted EBITDA to grow 17.3% YoY at the midpoint of its guidance, $435 million, implying an adjusted EBITDA margin expansion to 29.2% compared to 28.5% in FY 2025. At the same time, the company is guiding for GAAP income to grow 53.6% YoY at the midpoint, $125 million, which also implies a net margin expansion to 8.4% compared to 6.3% in FY 2025, highlighting the scale of its operating leverage amidst the anticipated top-line pressure.

Expanding Network to Drive Earnings Upside

With Pagaya guiding for network volume growth in FY 2026 despite shedding $1.5 billion in volume due to tightening its model, it should be noted that the primary growth driver for the company is its expanding network. From Q4 2025 up until its Q4 earnings call in late February, Pagaya onboarded three new partners to its network.

These partners include Achieve for its personal loan network, Global Lending Services for its auto loan network, and an unnamed major buy-now-pay-later (BNPL) provider for its POS network. Since then, the company has onboarded another partner to its network in early March, with another four or five partners in the pipeline to be onboarded this year.

With Pagaya exiting 2025 with 30 lending partners in its network, these new partners could push its network to nearly 40 lending partners by the end of the year. Accordingly, these new partners will be crucial for the company achieving its network volume guidance following its decision to tighten its model.

On that note, the impact of these new partners on Pagaya's network volume is likely to be minimal this year due to the typical ramp-up period required to fully integrate and scale loan volumes from new partner originations. This is a promising fact in light of management's guidance for 15% YoY network volume growth this year, as it suggests that growth could further accelerate in FY 2027 as these new partnerships mature.

Another positive aspect of Pagaya's partner expansion is its ability to offer its entire product suite to new partners immediately. As mentioned earlier, around 50% of the company's network volume in Q4 came from products other than its core Decline Monetization. These products include Affiliate Optimizer Engine, Direct Marketing Engine, and Dual Look, all of which are designed to help partners gain new customers and increase their loan conversion rates.

In my opinion, the initial lower network volumes from new partners and the lower margin profile of Pagaya's newer products reinforce my view of its FRLPC as a percentage of network volume trending near the midpoint of its 4-5% target in FY 2026. That said, I expect the company's FRLPC as a percentage of network volume could approach the higher end of its target range by late FY 2027 as its new partnerships mature and more partners adopt its newer products.

Despite the expected FRLPC margin pressure in FY 2026, the evident scale of Pagaya's operating leverage means that a majority of the incremental FRLPC growth in FY 2026 is likely to flow directly to its bottom line, supporting management's guidance for 53.6% YoY GAAP income growth. As is, the company's non-GAAP core operating expenses have been constant over the past 2 quarters at $47 million, highlighting its efficient cost management.

On another note, Pagaya has the potential to report lower than forecasted credit-related impairments in FY 2026, potentially driving further earnings upside, even with the anticipated higher volume from its new partners and the higher adoption of its product suite. With the company becoming more selective in the loans it approves, the incremental network volume from its new partners is likely to be for lower-risk borrower pools.

This suggests that its 2026 loan vintages are likely to outperform its loan vintages from the second half of 2024 through the first half of 2025, which are already outperforming its 2023 and prior vintages. With that in mind, Pagaya is likely to see a major reduction to its 2023 and prior loan vintages this year, considering that its personal loans typically carry terms of 36 and 60 months, meaning that a significant portion of these vintages will mature this year. In my opinion, the balance of Pagaya's 2023 and prior loan vintages could decline by 30% this year, thus reducing its exposure to these high-risk vintages.

Pagaya's expected lower exposure to 2023 and prior loan vintages combined with a conservative approach to approving loans this year suggests that it could report lower credit-related impairments this year compared to last year, which already came below management's forecast. Since the company's FY 2026 guidance is based on $100-150 million in credit-related impairments, reporting lower credit-related impairments than last year could push Pagaya's GAAP income near the high end of its guidance, $150 million, or even past its guidance.

Tightening Private Credit is an Opportunity

On another note, Pagaya has the potential to benefit from the ongoing weakness in the private credit market. The tightening private credit market is driven by its high exposure to the tech sector, driving fears among investors regarding valuations and transparency following the high-profile bankruptcies of First Brands and Tricolor late last year.

Additionally, fears of AI disrupting traditional software business models raised investors' fears of these companies repaying their private credit loans. As is, the use of paid-in-kind (PIK) interest is increasing, and $620 billion in debt issued in the low-rate environment of 2020 and 2021 is expected to be refinanced at a materially higher cost between 2026 and 2028.

These growing concerns have led to a spike in redemption requests from investors across several flagship funds, including Blue Owl (OWL), a key Pagaya funding partner with an active $2.4 billion forward flow agreement that expires in early 2027. Although private credit's direct lending arms are suffering, asset-backed financing (ABF) is emerging as an area of growth to offset direct lending's weakness.

According to Moody's 2026 Private Credit Outlook, private credit investments are shifting from corporate lending to ABF, especially in consumer loans and data infrastructure credit. This shift is expected to lead the private credit market's growth this year as ABF loans offer lower credit risk compared to direct corporate lending.

ABF loans' lower credit risk profile stems from the risk being spread across a vast loan pool as well as being backed by tangible assets with predictable cash flows. On the other hand, direct corporate lending's credit risk is centered on borrowers' EV and cash flow, and these loans are backed by corporate assets whose values depend on businesses' ongoing operations.

Against this backdrop, it appears that Pagaya is benefiting from this shift thanks to the volume of its ABS transactions so far this year. Year-to-date, Pagaya has raised $3.5 billion spanning 7 ABS transactions under its personal loan PAID program and auto loan RPM program. This suggests that Pagaya is on track to report another record year in terms of ABS funding, as its raised amount YTD represents 41% of the $8.5 billion it raised in FY 2025.

More crucially, all of Pagaya's ABS transactions this year included the participation of new investors. In fact, the company's PAID 2026-1 transaction, which was oversubscribed even after being upsized from $600 million to $800 million, attracted 4 new investors, the same number of investors the company added to its ABS network in the entirety of Q4 2025.

In my opinion, Pagaya's ability to attract new investors amidst private credit's ongoing jitters could be tied to its high repeat borrower rate. In its Q4 presentation, the company shared that 25-30% of its enabled loans are to borrowers who had a prior Pagaya-enabled loan in the last 3 years. This means that a significant portion of the company's network volume in 2025 is anchored by borrowers who were stress-tested during the 2022-2023 period that is still representing a drag on the company's financials.

This significantly de-risks Pagaya's loan vintages from the second half of 2024 and thereafter, in my opinion, since it already has the repayment history of these borrowers. Given that a repeat borrower's probability of default is statistically lower than a first-time borrower with the same FICO score, Pagaya can approve loans to these borrowers with higher confidence and lower credit enhancement requirements for its ABS transactions. As such, Pagaya's high repeat borrower rate reduces its credit risk while lowering its cost of capital, positioning it to report lower credit-related impairments on its second half of 2024 and thereafter loan vintages.

Additionally, Pagaya's ABS transactions this year included two resecuritization transactions under its PAID program and the inaugural resecuritization transaction under its RPM program, accounting for $1.2 billion of its total ABS issuances. These resecuritizations have a favorable risk profile for investors, as the loans included in these transactions typically have a weighted average seasoning of around 24 months, meaning that these loans have an established performance history.

Since these resecuritization transactions lower Pagaya's risk retention requirements on the resecuritized loans, they effectively reduce its cost of capital, leading to an improved FRLPC. Furthermore, these transactions unlock capital invested in lower-quality loans originated in the first half of 2024 and prior, thus allowing Pagaya to reallocate these funds to new, higher-quality network volume this year, further reducing its credit risk.

Valuation

In terms of valuation, Pagaya appears to be trading at a massive discount relative to other fintechs despite its robust fundamentals. At its share price of $13.59, Pagaya is trading at a non-GAAP P/E of 4.75 relative to analysts FY 2026 EPS target of $2.86, though these estimates could be extremely conservative given the company's expected network volume growth and strong operating leverage.

In comparison, companies like Upstart (UPST), SoFi (SOFI), LendingClub (LC), Affirm (AFRM), and Sezzle (SEZL) are trading at the following forward non-GAAP P/E ratios.

Furthermore, Pagaya is trading significantly below its sector median non-GAAP P/E of 23.78. This suggests that Pagaya is trading at an 80% discount to its sector median and a 74.5% discount to the average P/E of the aforementioned peer group.

With that in mind, the following YCharts shows that Pagaya's current non-GAAP forward P/E is near the bottom it reached in the first half of 2025, despite its improved fundamentals over that period.

In my opinion, Pagaya's current discount suggests a potential re-rating in the near term to reach a non-GAAP P/E between 8 and 10. In that scenario, my target multiple is 8, which implies a price target of $23, representing 68% upside from current levels.

Such a re-rating could be driven by Pagaya's upcoming Q1 earnings results on May 7th if it exceeds its network volume guidance between $2.5-2.7 billion and its GAAP income guidance between $15-35 million.

Meanwhile, if Pagaya beats its guidance, raises its full-year guidance, and loosens its model, indicating an improved macro outlook, the stock could re-rate to a forward non-GAAP P/E between 12 and 14, a level it has reached multiple times over the past 2 years. In that scenario, my target multiple is 12, which implies a price target of $34, representing 153% upside potential from current levels.

However, if Pagaya's Q1 results are within guidance while maintaining its full-year guidance or if new partners' ramps are slower than expected, the stock could drop post earnings and test the $10 support level. This scenario implies a potential 26% downside from the stock's share price.

Technical Analysis

My view of a Pagaya re-rating in the near term is further supported by its stock's price action. The TrendSpider chart above shows that Pagaya is in the midst of a textbook fishhook reversal pattern on the weekly. This structure can be seen with the stock consolidating near the $10.40 52-week low it reached post the Q4 earnings selloff, followed by a steady recovery towards the key $15 resistance level, accompanied with the RSI curving upward after dipping into oversold territory.

While the stock failed to break out of the $15 resistance twice over the last 2 trading weeks, the probability of a strong weekly close above that level is high, in my opinion, with the MACD line on the verge of a bullish crossover above the signal line, as the histogram reflects weakening selling momentum. Such a crossover could trigger a breakout of the $15 resistance and suggest a move to test the $20 resistance, which coincides with the company's upcoming Q1 earnings release.

Risks

Although I remain bullish on Pagaya's fundamentals and its near-term reversal potential, there are several risks worth noting that could undermine my thesis. From a macro level, March's CPI spike to 3.3% compared to 2.4% in February, driven by a 10.9% MoM surge in energy costs, is a key risk to watch as it could impact Pagaya's cost of capital and network volume. If higher energy costs persist, causing inflation to remain elevated above the Fed's 2% target, current expectations of a rate cut later this year may not materialize, while also introducing the risk of potential rate hikes to curb high inflation.

In that case, a wave of layoffs could push unemployment rates above 4.5%, which would increase the probability of default across Pagaya's loan pools and thus prolong Pagaya's credit tightening timeline. Moreover, Pagaya could be forced to increase the note coupon on its ABS transactions to drive institutional demand, which would reduce its take rate and effectively compress its FRLPC margin, which would eliminate any gains brought by its operating leverage scale.

Another macro risk worth noting is persistent weakness in the private credit market. While Pagaya appears to be benefiting from the current environment, if redemption requests remain elevated at Blue Owl specifically, the private credit firm could be forced to reduce loan purchases under the active $2.4 billion forward flow agreement to free up liquidity. Such an environment may also drive Blue Owl to not renew the current forward flow agreement with Pagaya when it expires in early 2027. In both cases, Pagaya's risk exposure would effectively increase if it is unable to secure similar agreements with other institutions.

As for Pagaya itself, a key risk to watch over the coming quarters is FRLPC margin dilution following its decision to tighten its model. If a majority of the company's network volume comes from its newer products, its FRLPC margin could trend below the midpoint of its target. Similarly, a slower-than-expected ramp-up of Pagaya's new partners may also dilute its FRLPC margin below the midpoint of its target, especially after shedding $1.5 billion in expected network volume this year. In both cases, any incremental network volume gains may not reflect on Pagaya's FRLPC in dollar terms, thus limiting any potential earnings upside from the company's operating leverage scale.

Conclusion

In summary, Pagaya presents an attractive risk-to-reward profile with its current valuation reminiscent of last year's despite its improved fundamentals over that period. While the company tightened its model in Q4, the scale of its operating leverage shone bright, with accelerating GAAP income and free cash flow generation sequentially despite top-line pressures.

Pagaya's operating leverage scale is set to be the primary driver for its anticipated bottom-line growth, as its top-line is expected to witness similar pressure this year due to its expanding partner network and the increasing adoption of its newer products. While these factors could compress Pagaya's FRLPC margin as a percentage of network volume, incremental FRLPC gains in dollar terms could mostly flow through to its bottom line thanks to its operating leverage scale.

On another note, Pagaya is emerging as a beneficiary from ongoing private credit woes in direct corporate lending, with private credit funds shifting to ABF structures due to their lower risk profile. This structural shift can be seen in the volume of the company's ABS transactions this year, representing 41% of its ABS funding in FY 2025, all of which saw the participation of new investors.

Furthermore, Pagaya's strong resecuritization momentum this year, raising $1.2 billion YTD, could be a crucial factor in reducing its credit-related impairments this year. With the company reducing its exposure to loans originated in 2023 and prior through resecuritizations and tightening its model, I expect it to report credit-related impairments lower than last year's $95.4 million.

Considering that the company's FY 2026 guidance is based on a credit-related impairment forecast between $100 million and $150 million, such an outperformance could see Pagaya exceed its full-year GAAP income guidance of between $100 million and $150 million. In light of these factors, I'm reiterating my strong buy rating for Pagaya with a base case price target of $23, implying 68% upside potential from its current valuation.

data.title: Heartland Small Cap Value Plus Strategy Portfolio Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Executive Summary

  • After market breadth began to improve late last year, the economy has been showing signs of broadening out.
  • However, the outbreak of war in Iran and rising oil prices raises questions of whether this trend may be hitting a pause.
  • In uncertain times like this, we are looking for signs that give us confidence in our security selection, such as stock buybacks, insider buying, and dividend increases.

First Quarter Market Discussion

Improving market breadth, which started to take shape at the end of last year, carried into the first quarter, with the Russell 2000® Index up 0.89% versus the S&P 500 Index which fell 4.33%.

The outbreak of war in Iran, however, seems to have clouded the picture. Since the military action began at the end of February, the broad market and small-cap companies have begun to slump. Yet history tells us not to overreact to short-term and unpredictable geopolitical developments. Time is better spent focusing on those factors that actually drive the markets in the long run. On that basis, there remains hope.

For instance, the underlying economy now appears to be broadening as well, confirming what improved market breadth had been signaling. Heading into the year, there were concerns about the uneven nature of the recovery, with high-net-worth households thriving thanks in part to growing asset values, while working class consumers have been struggling due to persistent inflationary pressures and affordability concerns. This divide has been referred to as the ‘K-shaped’ recovery.

Anecdotally, however, some companies that serve households in the bottom part of the K have reported seeing signs of green shoots forming this year. At the very least, some of the stresses on this segment of the economy no longer appear to be worsening. Lower-income households should also see a lift with the impending arrival of tax rebate checks, which disproportionately benefit the working class, though rising prices at the pump since the start of the war may have a counteracting effect.

Another sign of the broadening economy can be found in the health of the Industrials sector. For more than two years, the Institute for Supply Management’s Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) has been indicating contraction in factory activity. So far this year, though, the PMI for manufacturing has enjoyed back-to-back readings above 50, marking the first signs of growth in back-to-back months in four years. Even better, 90% of the individual PMI readings across various parts of the economy signaled strength during the first quarter. These are levels we have not seen since early 2022 (see the chart below).

Source: FactSet Research Systems Inc. (FDS) via Piper Sandler Monthly data 12/31/1975 to 3/31/2026. The data in this chart shows a diffusion index highlighting how many of the US PMIs (Dallas, Richmond, Philly, Chicago, Kansas, Empire) are currently in expansion relative to the total possible number. Regional US Purchasing Managers' Indices (PMIs) are monthly, localized economic indicators surveying manufacturing or service sector purchasing managers about business conditions. The ISM Services Index (formerly Non-Manufacturing Index or NMI) is a monthly economic indicator based on surveys of purchasing managers across U.S. The ISM Manufacturing Index, or Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI), is a monthly economic indicator based on surveys of purchasing managers at roughly 300–400 U.S. manufacturing firms. The S&P Global US Services PMI (formerly Markit) is a key monthly economic indicator gauging the private service sector's health, with a January 2026 reading of 52.70, indicating expansion. The S&P Global (formerly Markit) US Manufacturing PMI indicates the health of the manufacturing sector, with a January 2026 reading of 52.4, signaling expansion. All indices are unmanaged. It is not possible to invest in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

However, geopolitical risks could pose a threat to the trends that have been working in favor of small-cap investors. In times of uncertainty, it’s important to be confident in the companies we choose. Capital allocation decisions are insightful here. A good indicator of management’s conviction in their operational performance, strategic plan, and financial decision-making can come in the form of share buybacks, dividend increases and insider buying. Nearly 90% of the companies in our portfolio have active buybacks while 60% are currently boosting their dividend payouts and more than 1/3rd have seen insider buying. In all, 69% of our holdings are flashing at least two of these three important signs.

Another source of confidence comes from our adherence to our 10 Principles of Value Investing™. These guidelines require us to focus on fundamentals factors such valuations, balance sheet strength, earnings dynamics, and sound business strategies, among other considerations. Low prices, low debt, and high earnings growth potential help build in a margin of safety that is attractive to value investors like us but is particularly useful when uncertainty and volatility are on the rise.

Attribution Analysis & Portfolio Activity

The Value Plus Strategy gained 4.94% in the first quarter, compared with the 4.96% gain for the Russell 2000® Value Index. Stock selection accounted for some of our performance in the quarter, as our selection effect was positive in several sectors, led by Information Technology, Consumer Discretionary, and Health Care. By contrast, our selection effect was negative in Financials, Energy, and Real Estate.

The first quarter was a challenging period for artificial intelligence stocks, many of which stumbled as risk aversion returned to the market amid fears of a possible AI bubble forming. But for small-cap tech stocks, the first quarter proved to be quite strong in part because this group had already suffered setbacks last year and, for the most part, valuations never got out of hand.

An example is Silicon Motion Technology Corporation (SIMO). A year ago, shares of the leading maker of memory components used in PCs, smartphones, data centers, and industrial and auto applications sold off amid a variety of concerns. They included tariffs, consumer spending worries, and questions over whether investors might be overestimating the capex needs of large-scale cloud service providers known as hyperscalers. At the time, we remained committed to the stock because we believed the company was in the early days of a re-rating process, as SIMO had been making a push away from trailing-edge, lower-margin consumer electronics into higher-margin, leading-edge applications driven by hyperscaler demands.

What a difference a year makes. In the first quarter, the stock was a contributor to our outperformance, as consumer spending has held up and hyperscalers continue to indicate robust datacenter capex growth. In their fourth-quarter conference call, management reiterated the firm’s outlook for their PC and smartphone end markets and the growth prospects for their data center storage components, which are expected to drive margins substantially higher.

Yet in our opinion, SIMO remains meaningfully undervalued versus our current price target. The stock currently trades at $117, but we believe the company should be valued at $185. That’s based on a multiple of 15X EBITDA plus an anticipated $160 million cash settlement from SIMO’s ongoing arbitration with MaxLinear (MXL) surrounding the termination of a proposed merger agreement more than two years ago.

In this K-shaped recovery, shares of Wyndham Hotels (WH) have been punished as revenues for hotels that operate in the midscale and economy space have lagged behind upscale peers such as Marriott (MAR) or Hilton (HLT). This isn’t so surprising, as inflation has eaten into the consumer budgets while the weak manufacturing economy also impacted Wyndham’s grey and blue collar business travel base.Yet a broadening economy and improving PMI should benefit Wyndham’s base of business travelers and middle income customers. The global franchisor of hotels such as Wyndham, Days Inn, Laquinta, Ramada, and Super 8 should also see an incremental boost in demand in some of its key markets thanks to key events this year, including the World Cup in North America, the 250th birthday celebration for the U.S., and the 100th anniversary of Route 66.

Wyndham shares are valued at just 11-12x EBITDA, which compares favorably to Marriott and Hilton, which trade at 17-20x EBITDA. Even better, management has been actively buying back the company’s shares while consistently boosting its dividend payout, hitting two of our three capital allocation priorities.

One of the biggest detractors to our strategy’s performance in the quarter was Stifel Financial Corp. (SF), which provides brokerage, investment banking, investment advisory, and related financial services.

Shares of the global wealth manager began selling off in early February as the market expressed concerns around AI’s ability to disrupt their business. Then, the spike in interest rates, driven by concerns about higher inflation and oil prices, further weighed on the stock as the market sold off in March, impacting total client assets and investment banking prospects during a period of potentially “higher for longer” rates.

We believe Stifel should still be able to grow revenues and expand margins for the foreseeable future as the company continues to execute on organic asset growth. The company ranked no. 1 in JD Power’s Advisor Satisfaction study for the third consecutive year. As a result, Stifel continues to attract new financial advisors to its platform. Meanwhile, AI should be a tailwind for operational efficiency as it allows Stifel to automate routine tasks like compliance checks, client onboarding, and producing reports. This should help boost advisor productivity and better client retention.

Yet with price/earnings ratio of 11 times 2026 consensus EPS estimates, we believe Stifel is trading at an attractive valuation, especially for a business enjoying durable low-double-digit EPS growth.

Outlook

While recent geopolitical developments have introduced new uncertainties to the markets, investing is never certain. Our job as active managers is to focus on our security selection process. Our 10 Principles of Value Investing™ help guide us to underappreciated and overlooked stocks, many of which have already taken their lumps and trade at attractive levels. They also direct us to well-managed businesses with sound balance sheets and strategies, which build in an added margin of safety that will be particularly useful if volatility persists. If uncertainty wanes, though, we believe our bottom-up stock selection process should position us to take advantage of the broadening economy and improving market breadth.

Thank you for your continued trust and confidence in us.

References

data.title: Calamos Convertible Fund Q1 2026 Contributors And Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Calamos Convertible Fund Q1 2026 Commentary.

Contributors

Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE)

0.375% Convertible due 2032 | 5.4% of fund as of 3/31/26

Profile: Lumentum Holdings Inc. is a leading designer and manufacturer of optical and photonic products that are essential to the cloud, artificial intelligence and machine learning, telecommunications, and industrial end-market applications. AI/ML is providing a significant tailwind for the company, driving a dramatic surge in demand for Lumentum's optical components and modules. The company was incorporated in 2015 and is headquartered in San Jose, California.

Analysis: Lumentum Holdings' common stock and convertible advanced as investors increasingly recognized the company's central role in the buildout of AI and cloud data centers, where demand for high-speed optical components and photonic interconnects continued to accelerate. Momentum strengthened following strong fiscal second quarter results and upbeat guidance in early February, which highlighted sharp year-over-year revenue growth, expanding margins, and rising orders from hyperscale customers investing heavily in AI infrastructure. The stock also benefited from high-profile strategic developments that came to light later in the quarter, including a deepening partnership and a $2B investment from Nvidia. Lumentum also announced that it would join the S&P 500. Lumentum's convertible bond provides continued upside participation with a yield advantage over the common stock, which does not pay a dividend.

Western Digital Corp. (WDC)

3.00% Convertible due 2028 | 3.3% of fund as of 3/31/26

Profile: Western Digital Corp. is a leading global provider of data storage solutions, specializing in hard disk drives (HDDs), which have become critical components in the worldwide data infrastructure market. HDDs provide reliable, cost-effective, high-capacity storage for a wide range of applications, ranging from cloud data centers, enterprise storage systems and edge computing. The company primarily leverages its capabilities in the cloud and hyperscale data center markets. It was founded in 1970 and is headquartered in San Jose, California.

Analysis: Western Digital's common stock and convertible rallied during the quarter as the company reported strong earnings and raised its guidance. In addition, the company was added to the Nasdaq-100 Index ahead of year-end, which triggered inflows from passive institutional portfolios, adding a momentum boost to the stock. The company has continued to benefit since strategically separating its Flash (NAND/SSD) business, allowing it to sharpen its focus on high-capacity hard disk drives (HDDs) and capitalize on lucrative AI-driven storage demand. Its leadership in advanced UltraSMR technology and an aggressive product roadmap enable Western to capitalize on accelerating demand for AI-driven infrastructure and storage. The company also owns ~5% of SanDisk (SNDK), a stake valued at more than $2 billion. Western Digital's balance sheet is improving, and the convertible offers participation in the underlying stock with a favorable yield advantage.

Detractors

Snowflake Inc. (SNOW)

0.00% Convertible due 2029 | 1.3% of fund as of 2/28/26

Profile: Snowflake Inc. provides a cloud-based data platform that enables customers to consolidate data, build data applications, and apply AI to solve business problems and gain meaningful insights. The company collaborates with OpenAI to develop AI solutions for joint enterprise customers that deliver tangible returns on investment. Snowflake Inc. was incorporated in 2012 and is based in Menlo Park, California.

Analysis: During the quarter, Snowflake's common stock declined as investors expressed disappointment regarding the company's FY27 guide and concerns about AI's threat to SNOW's business model. During the quarter, the company's product revenue growth decelerated, and management's forward guidance implied a further moderation from prior high growth levels. Investors are concerned that increased competition from hyperscalers and data platform rivals might pressure Snowflake's long-term growth trajectory. We view Snowflake's convertible bond as an asymmetric way to participate in potential equity upside with measured downside risk.

Alphatec Holdings Inc. (ATEC)

0.75% Convertible due 2032 | 0.7% of fund as of 2/28/26

Profile: Alphatec Holdings Inc. is a medical technology company that develops technologies for the surgical treatment of spinal disorders. The company was founded in 1990 and is headquartered in Carlsbad, California.

Analysis: Alphatec Holdings' common stock and convertible declined after the company issued conservative 2026 EBITDA guidance, which implied less margin expansion relative to prior years due to investments to expand its sales force. We view Alphatec as an attractive medical technology company that has recently moved up to #3 in US market share by rapidly growing through product innovation. Recent financial release excepted, the company has grown substantially since new management took the reins in 2017. The company has achieved this growth by capturing share following the launch of a new product platform, in addition to pioneering a new surgical approach. We expect Alphatec to continue to grow above market and become a much bigger player in the global spine market. The convertible offers a favorable yield advantage over Alphatec's common stock as well as an attractive risk-reward profile.

data.title: JEPQ Vs. QQQI: Why I Favor The Passive Fund
data.description:
JEPQ vs. QQQI ETF: previous thesis and new developments

I last wrote on the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) on Feb. 12 in an article titled "JEPQ Vs. QQQ: February Effect Makes Covered-Call Funds Untimely". The article rated JEPQ as a hold after examining the historical volatility data in Feb. As for the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI), my last writing dates further back to Jan. 27. I rated QQQI also as a hold after comparing the volatility and valuation risks of Nasdaq-based funds against those based on the SP500 index.

Since those analyses, there have been a few notable changes both in terms of macroeconomic parameters and also fund specifics. The major ones that I will detail next are the valuation changes per their latest fund disclosures, their yield change due to their new dividend declarations, and also the changes of the volatility in the broader market. These changes have motivated me to compare JEPQ and QQQI, two funds that both provide a defensive layering for Nasdaq-100 exposure. More specifically, in the rest of this article, I will A) explain the top similarities and differences between these two funds and B) argue why the differences make QQQI a superior selection under current market conditions. In the end, these considerations have led me to maintain my hold rating on JEPQ and upgrade my rating on QQQI to buy.

JEPQ vs. QQQI ETF: Basic fund facts

Before diving in further, let's take a moment to review some basic fund facts for readers new to these funds. As just mentioned, both the JEPQ and QQQI funds are based on the Nasdaq-100 index and both provide a defensive layering via the use of derivative-based income generation as detailed in their fund description below.

JEPQ Fund description: JEPQ actively invests in US large-cap stocks comprising its benchmark, the Nasdaq-100 Index, while pursuing lower volatility. The fund may also invest up to 20% of the portfolio in equity-linked notes (ELNs) issued by counterparties, including banks, broker-dealers or their affiliates, to provide additional income.

QQQI Fund description: QQQI strategically invests in the Nasdaq-100 Index stocks and utilizes NDX call options to deliver high monthly income through dividends and option premiums. It executes a call-writing approach, possibly complemented by purchasing long, out-of-the-money calls to achieve a net credit.

But there are also many differences. To start, JEPQ boasts a far larger asset base of almost $37 billion and also a lower expense ratio of 0.35% compared to QQQI's $11 billion AUM and 0.68% fee (see the next chart below). More fundamentally, JEPQ is an active fund. JEPQ's fund managers pick its holdings from the NASDAQ universe through a proprietary data-driven algorithm, while QQQI's holdings are simply based on the underlying index.

As a result, there are some noticeable differences in their holdings, as you can see from the following chart. Certainly, JEPQ's and QQQI's holdings share many overlaps. After all, they are drawn from the same Nasdaq-100 Index, which itself is concentrated heavily on a handful of mega-cap technology stocks. As an example, among their top 10 holdings, many of them overlap. Due to such a shared DNA, both ETFs generally experience the same directional movements (more on this later), which is also similar to the underlying index and also the broader tech market. Although there are also differences. For example, in their top 10 holdings, JEPQ holds Micron (MU), but QQQI does not. Vice versa, QQQI holds Walmart (WMT) as its 10th largest position, while JEPQ does not. And among the tickers that do overlap, the weights are different.

Next, I will explain why these differences lead to a more favorable return/risk curve for QQQI under current market conditions.

JEPQ vs. QQQI ETF: Valuation and yield

The first key result brought about by the above differences in holdings is valuation risk. Per JEPQ's latest disclosure, as shown in the next screenshot (dated on March 31, 2026), the holdings in JEPQ now actually trade at a higher price-to-earnings ratio of 21.75x compared to the Nasdaq index's 20.74x, which itself is quite elevated for reasons detailed in our last JEPQ article. Also note that JEPQ exhibits a high turnover ratio of 168% (per its June 30, 2025, data), a point I will revisit later.

Of course, with the Nasdaq-100 index hovering near an all-time high, the valuation of QQQI's holdings is also elevated. However, I believe QQQI's valuation risks are better offset by several factors. Firstly and simply, its P/E multiples should be lower than JEPQ's as QQQI's holdings duplicated the underlying index. Secondly, QQQI's use of option overlay is also different from JEPQ, which makes QQQI more effective for capital preservation and income generation in my experience. Overall, my interpretation of their option strategy is that A) JEPQ's strategy is more dynamic (given it's an active fund), which could leave a portion of the portfolio unhedged to capture more upside, whereas B) QQQI uses a systematic index spread approach that is designed to capture high premiums regardless of market direction. This difference has led to higher yield from QQQI, both in the past and also in the present. For example, as shown in the next chart, QQQI is currently yielding 14.04% TTM with its latest payout announcement, more than 1/3 higher than JEPQ's 10.48%. QQQI's historical average yield of 10.9% is also more than 17% higher than JEPQ's 9.29%.

Other risks and final thoughts

There is nothing inherently wrong with the tradeoff that JEPQ strikes between upside potential and current income. It is just that I consider the current juncture to be a bad time to pursue price appreciation given the elevated valuation ratios of the broader market and also ongoing geopolitical risks. In particular, I believe investors are underestimating market volatility currently. As a reflection, the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) chart below illustrates market expectations of near-term volatility in the past year. As seen, the current reading of 18.82 hovers closely around the long-term average of 18.49. Yet, I see many geopolitical risks that should cause the VIX to be significantly above average, ranging from the uncertainties in the Middle East to energy prices. In the case of a volatility surge, QQQI's higher dividend yield and systematic spread approach could provide a thicker downside cushion than JEPQ.

To echo an observation made earlier, as an active fund, JEPQ's turnover rate is quite high at 168%. QQQI's turnover rate is relatively high also at 22% as seen in the next chart when compared to vanilla indexed funds with no option overlay. As a reference point, QQQ's turnover rate is in the upper single digits (around 8%), and SPY's is even lower at around 3%. In the long term, a higher turnover rate tends to suffer more trading costs and frictions.

To close, this article is mainly motivated by changes in the macroeconomics and also the latest fund updates disclosed by both JEPQ and QQQI funds. Changes in the former category included the geopolitical conflicts in the Middle East, the changes in energy pricing, and the VIX Index. Changes in the later category included these funds' holdings, valuation ratios, and dividend yields. These changes have made me conclude that now is a bad time to pursue price appreciation and prefer a more defensive stance. Between JEPQ and QQQI, QQQI's holdings and way of option overlay fit my preference. Due to the reasons mentioned above, both funds tend to move in the same directions as shown in the chart below. However, as also shown in the chart, historically, QQQI recovered more quickly from drawdowns after volatility surged both in 1H 2025 and also in 1H 2026. With my above outlook for volatility and valuation risks, I see large odds for this pattern to repeat again if/when VIX surges again.

data.title: ECB's Latest Surveys Point To Rising Stagflationary Pressures
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

This morning’s ECB survey results suggest that stagflationary pressures have increased in the eurozone ahead of Thursday’s policy meeting

The European Central Bank just released the results of its very own bank lending survey as well as the consumer expectations survey. In short, the results of both surveys suggest that stagflationary pressures in the eurozone have clearly increased, complicating the ECB’s life not only at its meeting on Thursday but also beyond.

The Bank Lending Survey for the first quarter of 2026 shows a net tightening of credit standards for both consumer credit and company loans and weaker demand for new loans. At the same time, the consumer expectations survey shows an increase in inflation expectations in March across all time horizons, with inflation expectations for one year ahead moving to 4% year-on-year, from 2.5% YoY in February.

Ahead of Thursday’s ECB meeting, this morning’s data provides more evidence that the war in the Middle East and the rise in energy prices are not only posing an inflationary shock but rather a stagflationary shock for the eurozone economy. As much as the rise in inflation expectations will fuel the rate hike debate, growing signs of adverse growth effects will make aggressive rate hikes less straightforward. Even though the ECB’s primary policy goal is price stability, it’s hard to see that it would really want to fight an exogenous supply shock at the cost of worsening an economic downturn.

data.title: Allspring Emerging Markets Equity Advantage Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The fund outperformed the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (Net) for the quarter that ended March 31, 2026.
  • At the sector level, stocks within the financials, consumer staples, and consumer discretionary sectors served as relative contributors, while stocks within the industrials, information technology (IT), and utilities sectors hindered relative performance.
  • At the country level, stock selection in India, Brazil, and Peru aided relative performance, while stocks within South Africa, Taiwan, and Thailand impeded relative returns.

Market review

Emerging market equities, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (Net), declined 0.17% in the first quarter, as attractive relative valuations continued to result in outperformance versus developed markets. China/Hong Kong underperformed (-8.94%) the benchmark in the first quarter, as equities weakened amid the Middle East conflict, but still outperformed many peer markets. The National People's Congress set a conservative 4.5-5.0% growth target, signaling support for stability and innovation without aggressive stimulus as China enters the 15th Five-Year Plan. Taiwan equities advanced 9.09%, despite weak market performance in March due to profit-taking pressure following strong year-to-date gains amid uncertainty around global geopolitical risks. India's market had poor performance (-18.13%) on concerns related to higher prices and availability of crude oil and natural gas and its adverse effect on the economy. Outside of Asia, performance was mixed, with Latin America generally outperforming the broader MSCI Emerging Markets Index (Net). Brazil markets outperformed (19.10%) the benchmark as the government implemented a diesel subsidy funded by a temporary 11% crude export tax. Mexico also outperformed (7.68%) the benchmark, as formal bilateral meetings on the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement joint review began. Higher oil prices also benefited the energy sector of Middle Eastern countries, despite heavy conflict taking place in the region. Saudi Arabia outperformed (9.23%) the benchmark, and due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, the country is working on re-routing ~40-50% of its stranded Hormuz exports through its pipeline to the Red Sea.

Fund performance and attribution

  • Petroleo Brasileiro
  • Southern Copper Corp. (SCCO)
  • Telefonica Brasil (VIV)

DETRACTORS
