The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

Quarterly Sector Attribution Analysis

At the sector level, stocks within the financials, consumer staples, and consumer discretionary sectors served as relative contributors, while stocks within the industrials, IT, and utilities sectors hindered relative performance.

SK Hynix, the world's second largest memory chip producer, was a detractor in the first quarter, as it did not meet our yield requirements until the announcement of an enhanced shareholder return policy in late January 2026. This policy included a 2.1% Treasury share cancellation and an increase in the dividend from South Korean won (KRW) 2,200 to KRW 3,000 per share, supported by strong free cash flow generation that enabled us to build a position for the portfolio. With stronger than expected dynamic random access memory and NAND flash memory demand, persistent supply tightness, and artificial intelligence (AI)-driven infrastructure investment sustaining elevated memory pricing, we expect the share price to continue performing, with the stock trading at ~5 times 2026 price-to-earnings ((P/E)) and ~2.8 times price-to-book.

Telefonica Brasil, a leading provider of mobile and fixed telecommunication services, performed well and was a contributor after its fourth quarter earnings beat expectations. The company is delivering revenue growth above inflation while expanding free cash flows at a double-digit pace. Our recent meeting with management reinforced our conviction, as capital expenditure intensity decline is aided by a long runway for asset sales.

Quarterly Country Attribution Analysis

At the country level, stock selection in India, Brazil, and Peru aided relative performance, while stocks within South Africa, Taiwan, and Thailand impeded relative returns.

Southern Copper Corp., which is among the largest pure-play copper producers globally, performed strongly in the first quarter. The company benefited from record copper prices and a re-rating in shares due to expectations of the beginning of a structurally tight copper market. Fourth quarter 2025 results were strong and above expectations—earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) was up 53% year over year and achieved an EBITDA margin of 60%.

Wiwynn Corp., a leading global original design manufacturer of AI and traditional servers, underperformed the benchmark as its gross margin fell to 7.2% in the fourth quarter of 2025 (versus market expectations of 8%). This was driven by product mix shift toward higher average selling price AI server projects with lower margins. We remain positive on Wiwynn given its strong positioning to benefit from cloud service providers' rising investment in both AI and traditional servers. We expect Wiwynn to deliver sustainable 20–30% earnings growth over the next two to three years. Trading at 11 times P/E and a 4.7% dividend yield, we believe the stock is undervalued.

Fund positioning and outlook

After a very strong start to 2026, emerging market equities, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (Net), fell more than 13% in March to end slightly down for the quarter, as instability has spread in the Middle East on U.S./Israel and Iran conflict.

Despite near-term weakness and delayed easing from oil shock, we remain constructive on emerging markets due to improved political visibility, room for policy accommodation, economic growth premium versus developed markets, and low investor positioning. Macroeconomic impact from the energy crisis is manageable with the biggest drag on the Gulf Cooperation Council and some of the smaller/lower-income economies—for example, in Southeast Asia.

Emerging market central banks are still assessing inflation from high oil and are mostly on hold but not turning hawkish. Many emerging market governments have flexibility for fiscal adjustments and are introducing policies to cushion against fuel shortage/price spike. Emerging markets enter the current energy crisis with a solid macroeconomic backdrop, stronger fiscal health, and meaningfully improved governance compared with history. Strength in advanced manufacturing and export, particularly in tech AI supply chain as well as rich natural resources, provides an offset to pressure from an energy shortage. Even with downward revision to estimates, we continue to expect double-digit earnings growth and rising buybacks and dividends to drive returns.

We believe that our strategy, focusing on quality companies with robust shareholder yields, provides a resilient framework to weather the current volatility and invest in emerging markets for the long term.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. data.title: ASML: Potential Bull Trap As AI Super Cycle Continues - Reiterate Hold
data.description:
I previously rated ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) (ASMLF) as a Hold in February 2026, with the overly done rally/premium valuations already negating the robust increase in its bookings/backlogs for the next-gen lithography offerings.

In this article, I shall discuss why ASML remains a Hold despite the robust tailwind from the multi-year cloud spending trends.

ASML Proves Its AI Beneficiary Status

Since my last Hold rating, ASML has mostly traded sideways at +0.91% compared to the wider market at +2.7%, with it naturally lending credence to the caution offered in my last article.

The sluggish stock price movement is also indicative of the premium previously embedded in its stock valuations from the overly done January 2026 rally, despite the lifting AI sentiments as the market enters the promising Q1'26 earnings season.

1. Durability of AI Spending Trends

For now, the two godfathers of the AI boom, ASML and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), already reiterate the durability of the multi-year cloud super cycle as the six largest US hyperscalers also guide approximately $700B in capital expenditure in 2026, "nearly six times 2022 levels."

Particularly, the outsized spending trends are relatively well split between the logic and memory segments, as observed in ASML's net system sales, "almost equally split between Logic at 49% and Memory at 51%" in FQ1'26.

This development is unsurprising indeed, as TSM reports very tight capacity and a "higher overall capacity utilization rate," along with the growing demand for high-tech 3nm/5nm offerings at 61% of its FQ1'26 revenues (+3 points YoY).

Readers may also want to note that a similar cadence has been reported by Micron (MU), attributed to the "increase in memory demand driven by AI, structural supply constraints," along with the ongoing sampling of their next-gen HBM4 offering in 2026 and the volume ramp of HBM4E in 2027.

With the litho/semiconductor chip supply tightness expected to last through 2027, if not longer, it is unsurprising that ASML already hints at further growth opportunities as their customers accelerate "their capacity expansion plans for 2026 and beyond, supported by long-term agreements with their customers."

Therefore, while the management has yet to report the quarterly booking trends, their order intake is likely to be "very strong" in FQ1'26 while lending credence to their raised output of at least 60 low-NA EUV systems in 2026 (+25% YoY) and up to 80 systems in 2027 (+33% YoY), since this segment comprises the lion's share at 66% of its FQ1'26 revenues and 48% in FY2025.

Over the next few years, ASML is likely to enjoy further growth tailwinds from Tesla's (TSLA) and Intel's (INTC) upcoming Terafab chip production project.

This is attributed to their latter two's plans to "produce 1 TW/year [terawatt per year] of compute to power future advances in AI and robotics," up by "50 times the current global compute supply of around 20 GW."

These present ASML's undisputed AI beneficiary status across a growing group of customers and end markets, with it perhaps contributing to the premium embedded in its stock prices/valuations.

2. Higher Installed Base Monetization Trends

ASML also benefits from the expanded Installed Base Management sales at €2.5B (+25% YoY) or the equivalent of 28.3% of its overall revenues in FQ1'26 (+2.4 points YoY/+4.5 from FY2019 levels of 23.8%).

While the management does not break down the segment's gross margins, the company's overall gross margins of 53% (-1 points YoY/-8.3 from FY2019 levels of 44.7%) already benefited from "the contribution of very high-margin components within our installed base business."

This development is unsurprising indeed, attributed to the foundry customers' elevated utilization trends and the ongoing customers' productivity upgrading "for their installed base to increase their short-term output requirements."

These reasons are also why ASML's installed base management segment is likely to continue growing over the next few years, as the company increasingly monetizes their new and older equipment.

3. Raised FY2026 Guidance

ASML has further fed the optimism by raising the FY2026 total net sales guidance to €38B at the midpoint (+16.2% YoY, compared to the prior guidance at €36.5B (+11.6% YoY)) while keeping the gross margins guidance at 52% (-0.8 points YoY).

Combined with the FQ1'26 outperformance, it is unsurprising that the consensus has already raised their forward estimates, with the company expected to deliver an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +16.2%/+24.6% through FY2028.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +18.5%/+23.8% and a 10Y CAGR of +17.9%/+22.6%, respectively, with it underscoring ASML's beneficiary status during the multi-year AI infrastructure boom.

Readers may also want to note that these numbers are notably accelerated from the management's prior FY2030 revenue growth guidance range of between €44B and €60B (expanding at a 5Y CAGR of +9.7% at the midpoint), with the current FY2026 guidance and the consensus estimates tracking beyond to the upper end range at a 5Y CAGR of +12.9%.

4. Profitable Growth Trends Already Priced In

Thanks to the combination of the sideways trading since January 2026 and the raised consensus forward estimates, it is apparent that ASML is gradually growing into its premium valuations, as observed in the moderation from the prior FWD P/E highs of 47.85x to 39.88x at the time of writing, albeit still elevated from the 5Y mean of 37.52x and the 10Y mean of 31.80x

Based on the moderated FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 39.88x and the consensus raised adj. EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +24.6%, and the stock also looks more compelling at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.62x, compared to the prior highs of 1.94x, the 5Y mean of 1.74x, and the 10Y mean of 1.40x.

On the other hand, when comparing ASML's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 39.88x and my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.62x to its AI infrastructure godfathers, including:

  • AI chip accelerator leader: Nvidia (NVDA) at 24.97x/0.59x (using the same methodology),
  • Custom AI chip accelerator leader: Broadcom (AVGO) at 37x/0.75x,
  • foundry leader: TSM at 26.05x/0.79x, and
  • foundry/x86: INTC at 76.76x/1.06x, respectively,

It is apparent that ASML remains somewhat inflated at current levels, warranting investors waiting for a dip-buying opportunity.

The silver lining to ASML's investment thesis will be its sustainably healthy balance sheet at a net cash position of €5.67B in FQ1'26 (+4.8% YoY), as the management utilizes the rich cash flows to retire -1.7% of its float over the LTM and -8.5% from FY2019 levels.

This is on top of the rich dividend payouts at €7.50 per share in 2025 (+17.1% YoY) and the 5Y dividend growth rate at +21.9%, with the rich shareholder returns and the healthy balance sheet perhaps contributing to the premium embedded in its valuations.

ASML Remains A Hold Here

For now, ASML has failed to break out of the February 2026 resistance levels of $1.56K after the recent recovery in April 2026, with it presenting a potential double top, bull trap at current inflated levels.

This is especially since the stock is still trading at a premium compared to my fair value estimates of ~$1.15K, based on:

  • The 10Y P/E mean of 31.80x (nearer to peer group current P/E mean of ~31.9x, aside from the inflated INTC at 76x), and
  • The estimated FY2026 adj EPS of ~$36.30 (+22.9% YoY, based on the management's raised total net sales guidance to €38B at the midpoint (+16.2% YoY), the FQ1'26 net income margins of 31.3% (+1 point YoY), and the FQ1'26 shares outstanding at 385.7M (-1.7% YoY) along with the current exchange rate of €1 to $1.18).

Based on the consensus raised FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $56.07, ASML has also pulled forward part of its upside potential to my long-term price target of $1.78K.

With the FQ1'26 earnings call behind us and three more months to go to its upcoming FQ2'26 earnings call sometime in July 2026, I am of the opinion that ASML is likely to return part of its recent gains in the near term, worsened by the potential selling pressure after the ex-dividend date of April 27, 2026.

Combined with the potential volatility from the double top, bull trap at the current inflated levels, and reversing trading momentum, interested readers may be better off waiting for a moderate correction to the March 2026 bottom of $1.25K for an improved margin of safety, with those levels also nearer to my fair value estimates of $1.15Ks.

Given the projected -17.2% downside at the midpoint from the current levels of $1.45K, I urge some patience in the meantime and therefore reiterate my prior Hold rating for the ASML stock here.
